items.q: Are decisions made by algorithms automatically neutral and objective?
items.correct: No - algorithms inherit the assumptions of their designers and the biases in their training data, so they can systematically disadvantage groups of people.
items.misconception: Yes - computers have no opinions, so their decisions are always fair.
items.random_wrong: Yes - bias is impossible once a process is automated.
items.why: The machine's lack of emotion is mistaken for fairness. Bias enters through data and design choices, and automation can then apply it consistently at scale.

items.q: Does anonymising a dataset guarantee individuals can never be identified?
items.correct: No - combining an anonymised dataset with other available data can often re-identify individuals, which is why anonymisation alone is not a complete safeguard.
items.misconception: Yes - removing names makes re-identification impossible.
items.random_wrong: Yes - anonymised data is no longer data about people at all.
items.why: Stripping obvious identifiers feels final. Re-identification studies show that a few attributes - postcode, birth date, gender - can single out most individuals.

items.correct: No - automation displaces some roles while creating others, such as development, maintenance and data analysis; the effects are uneven across skills and regions.
items.misconception: Yes - every automated task is a job lost forever with nothing created.
items.random_wrong: Yes - automation has no effect on employment at all.
items.why: One-sided narratives dominate headlines. Balanced exam answers weigh displacement against new roles and discuss who bears the costs of transition.

items.q: Algorithmic bias most commonly arises when:
items.correct: A system is trained on data that reflects existing human prejudices
items.random_wrong: The screen brightness is set too high
items.why: It's A system is trained on data that reflects existing human prejudices. “A computer overheats during processing” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Creating or distributing malware to damage systems is criminalised in the UK by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Computer Misuse Act 1990. “The Highways Act” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which right do individuals have under UK data protection law?
items.correct: To access the personal data organisations hold about them
items.random_wrong: To force any website to close
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To access the personal data organisations hold about them. “To read anyone else's emails” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A widely discussed consequence of large-scale automation is:
items.correct: Displacement of routine jobs alongside the creation of new technical roles
items.misconception: The end of all electricity use
items.why: It's Displacement of routine jobs alongside the creation of new technical roles. “The end of all electricity use” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) come into force across the EU?
items.why: The real figure is 2018. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
